name: Camino Mozárabe and Guadalquivir Loop
name_original: Cordel de la Campiña – circular desde Córdoba por los puentes Romano y Carbonell
description: Hike the difficult 11.7-mile Camino Mozárabe and Guadalquivir Loop, gaining 708 feet and exploring historic bridges near Córdoba.
region_intro: Hiking around the Guadalquivir offers diverse landscapes, from the rugged mountains of the Sierra de Cazorla to the expansive wetlands of Doñana National Park. The region is characterized by its winding riverbanks, dense holm oak forests, and unique karst formations. Trails traverse deep valleys, fertile plains, and protected natural parks, providing varied terrain for outdoor exploration. This area presents a rich tapestry of natural features, suitable for various levels of outdoor enthusiasts. Best hiking trails around the Guadalquivir The most popular hiking route is Río Borosa Trail, a 11.5 miles (18.6 km) trail that takes 5 hours 57 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the rugged terrain of the Sierra de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. Another top favourite among local hikers is The four bridges of the Guadalquivir River — circular tour of the city of Sevilla, an easy 3.7 miles (5.9 km) path. This urban route offers scenic views of the river and historic landmarks in Seville. Local hikers also love the Embalse de la Breña - circular from Almodóvar del Río, a 7.1 miles (11.5 km) trail leading through reservoir landscapes, often completed in about 3 hours 11 minutes. Hiking around the Guadalquivir is defined by rugged mountains, extensive river systems, and diverse wetlands. The network offers options for both easy riverside strolls and more demanding mountain routes. The routes in Guadalquivir are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 1200 reviews. More than 10000 hikers have used komoot to explore Guadalquivir's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in the Guadalquivir region?
A: The Guadalquivir region offers a vast network of over 500 hiking trails. This includes a wide range of options, with more than 280 easy routes, over 200 moderate trails, and around 30 more challenging paths, ensuring there's something for every skill level.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking along the Guadalquivir?
A: Hiking along the Guadalquivir offers incredibly diverse landscapes. You can explore the rugged mountains and deep valleys of the Sierra de Cazorla, dense holm oak forests and karst formations in the Sierra Morena, or the expansive wetlands, lagoons, and dunes of Doñana National Park. There are also serene riverside paths and urban green spaces, all connected by the historic river.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in the Guadalquivir region?
A: Yes, the region has many family-friendly trails. For example, urban routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1010585269/the-four-bridges-of-the-guadalquivir-river-circular-tour-of-the-city-of-sevilla" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The four bridges of the Guadalquivir River — circular tour of the city of Sevilla&lt;/a&gt; offer easy walks with scenic views. The Sierra Norte de Sevilla Natural Park, within the Sierra Morena, also provides accessible nature walks suitable for families.

Q: Where can I find trails with waterfalls or dramatic natural features?
A: For trails featuring waterfalls and rugged terrain, head to the Sierra de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e980001275/rio-borosa-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Borosa Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a notable difficult route that explores this area, known for its deep valleys and the impressive Salto de los Órganos waterfall mentioned in the region's research.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes along the Guadalquivir?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. A popular easy option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1010585269/the-four-bridges-of-the-guadalquivir-river-circular-tour-of-the-city-of-sevilla" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The four bridges of the Guadalquivir River — circular tour of the city of Sevilla&lt;/a&gt;, which offers scenic urban views. For a more natural setting, consider the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995853178/embalse-de-la-brena-circular-from-almodovar-del-rio" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de la Breña - circular from Almodóvar del Río&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you around a reservoir.

Q: What are some interesting landmarks or attractions I can see near the hiking trails?
A: Many trails pass by significant landmarks and natural attractions. In Seville, you can explore historic sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4834476" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Royal Alcázar of Seville&lt;/a&gt; or enjoy green spaces like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2616148" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alamillo Park&lt;/a&gt;. Further afield, you might encounter natural features such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3168204" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco del Aceite&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/633842" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Breña Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, offering beautiful views and birdwatching opportunities.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in the Guadalquivir region?
A: The Guadalquivir region is best for hiking during the cooler months, typically from autumn (September-November) through spring (March-May). During these seasons, temperatures are more moderate, making for comfortable hiking conditions. Summers can be very hot, especially in the plains, so early mornings or late afternoons are recommended if hiking during that time.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly hiking trails?
A: While specific regulations can vary by protected area, many trails outside strict nature reserves are generally dog-friendly, especially if kept on a leash. The Sierra Norte de Sevilla Natural Park, within the Sierra Morena, is known for its accessible nature walks where dogs are often welcome. Always check local signage for specific restrictions, particularly in national parks like Doñana.

Q: Are permits required for any hiking routes in the Guadalquivir region?
A: Permits are generally not required for most standard hiking trails in the Guadalquivir region. However, access to certain highly protected areas, such as parts of Doñana National Park or specific sections within other natural parks, may require guided tours or special permits. It's always advisable to check with the local park authorities if you plan to venture into sensitive ecological zones.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Guadalquivir?
A: The trails in Guadalquivir are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 1200 reviews. Hikers often praise the region's unparalleled ecological diversity, ranging from high mountain origins to expansive river deltas, and the variety of terrain that caters to all fitness levels.

Q: Are there any long-distance or historical hiking routes?
A: Yes, the region includes sections of historical routes. The Camino Mozárabe, part of the pilgrimage routes to Santiago de Compostela, features sections that loop with the Guadalquivir, offering a blend of natural beauty and historical exploration. You can experience a part of this on the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e999792793/camino-mozarabe-and-guadalquivir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino Mozárabe and Guadalquivir Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find easy day hikes along the Guadalquivir River?
A: Absolutely. Many easy day hikes are available, particularly along the riverbanks and in urban green spaces. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1010605256/alcazar-of-sevilla-cathedral-of-santa-maria-and-setas-de-sevilla-walk-through-the-old-town-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alcazar of Seville, Cathedral of Santa Maria and Setas de Sevilla - walk through the old town - circular&lt;/a&gt; offers a pleasant stroll through Seville's historic center, while the Coria del Río waterfront also provides relaxing riverside walks.

Q: Are there opportunities for wild swimming along the hiking routes?
A: While the Guadalquivir River itself is not typically recommended for wild swimming due to currents and water quality variations, some natural areas and reservoirs might offer opportunities. For example, areas around &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3168204" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco del Aceite&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/633842" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Breña Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; could have designated spots for swimming, but always check local regulations and safety information before entering the water.

faqs.question: Where does the Camino Mozárabe and Guadalquivir Loop trail start?
faqs.answer: This loop trail typically starts and ends in Córdoba, offering convenient access to the city's amenities. You can find parking options within Córdoba, though specific trailhead parking isn't explicitly designated for this urban-adjacent loop. Public transport within Córdoba can also bring you close to various points along the route.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this hike?
faqs.answer: The Camino Mozárabe and Guadalquivir Loop is approximately 18.8 kilometers long and generally takes around 5 hours and 2 minutes to complete at a steady hiking pace. This duration does not include stops for sightseeing or breaks.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain on this specific loop is varied, incorporating riverside paths along the Guadalquivir, urban sections, and parts of the historic Camino Mozárabe. You'll encounter ancient Roman roads and medieval paths, but also modern trails like the Paseo de los Caballos. While the overall elevation gain is moderate (around 216m), the trail is rated as difficult, suggesting some challenging sections or uneven surfaces.

faqs.question: What notable sights will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: This route offers a blend of historical and natural highlights. You'll cross the iconic Roman Bridge of Córdoba and the Puente Carbonell over the Guadalquivir River. Other points of interest include the Molino de Carbonell and the unique sight of a Douglas DC-6 at the Balcón del Guadalquivir.

faqs.question: Is this loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Despite its relatively short distance, this loop is rated as difficult. While it offers accessible paths, the overall challenge might be significant for absolute beginners. Hikers should have a reasonable level of fitness and be prepared for varied terrain. If you're new to hiking, consider shorter, easier sections or other trails in the area first.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The best times to hike this route are during the cooler months of winter, early spring, or autumn. Summer in Andalusia can bring extreme heat, making hiking uncomfortable and potentially unsafe. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and often beautiful scenery.

faqs.question: Is the Camino Mozárabe and Guadalquivir Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are welcome on many trails in Spain, especially on less crowded routes. However, specific leash rules may apply in urban parks or protected areas along the loop. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved, on a leash where required, and that you carry bags to clean up after them. Be mindful of the heat, especially for pets, during warmer months.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike the Camino Mozárabe and Guadalquivir Loop. It is a publicly accessible route, though some attractions or facilities along the way might have their own entry fees.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with other routes?
faqs.answer: Yes, this loop incorporates sections of several other trails. Notably, it covers about 37% of the official Camino Mozárabe route. You'll also find yourself on parts of the Paseo de los Caballos, Calle Gaviones, and various local bike paths like Carril bici Estadio El Arcángel and Carril bici avda. Campo de la Verdad.

faqs.question: Are there places to find refreshments or accommodation nearby?
faqs.answer: As this loop starts and ends in Córdoba and passes through urban-adjacent areas, you'll find ample opportunities for refreshments, meals, and accommodation. Córdoba offers a wide range of cafes, restaurants, and hotels to suit various budgets, making it easy to plan your pre- or post-hike needs.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the 'difficult' rating and potential for varied terrain, it's advisable to pack sturdy hiking shoes, sufficient water (especially in warmer weather), sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and snacks. A basic first-aid kit is always recommended. While the trail is well-marked, a map or GPS device can be helpful for navigation.
